perturbation.original_text: Business Day means a day other than a Saturday, Sunday or bank or public holiday in England or Wales;
perturbation.changed_text: Business Day means any day of the week, including Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays.
perturbation.explanation: This modification alters the definition of 'Business Day' to include weekends and holidays. This contradicts the UK's statutory right to bank holidays, where businesses are generally closed, effectively forcing employees to work on these legally protected days. This introduces legal risk, because, while not directly mandating holiday closures, UK employment law provides protections and enhanced pay for working on bank holidays.
perturbation.law_explanation: By defining a business day to include all days, the contract undermines the implied rights and protections afforded to employees regarding bank holidays, potentially leading to claims related to unfair working conditions, lack of holiday pay, or coercion to work on public holidays. While not a direct contradiction of a specific clause mandating paid time off, it circumvents the spirit and intent of employment regulations designed to provide adequate rest and holiday entitlement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Holiday entitlement: Entitlement - GOV.UK Cookies on GOV.UK We use some essential cookies to make this website work. We’d like to set additional cookies to understand how you use GOV.UK, remember your settings and improve government services. We also use cookies set by other sites to help us deliver content from their services. You have accepted additional cookies. You can change your cookie settings at any time. You have rejected additional cookies. You can change your cookie settings at any time. Accept additional cookies Reject additional cookies View cookies Hide this message Skip to main content Holiday entitlement Skip contents Entitlement Almost all people classed as workers are legally entitled to 5.6 weeks’ paid holiday a year (known as statutory leave entitlement or annual leave). This includes: agency workers workers with ‘irregular hours’ (where the number of hours they work in a pay period often or always changes) ‘part-year’ workers (where there are periods of at least a week in a leave year where they do not need to work and are not paid) An employer can include bank holidays as part of statutory annual leave. Statutory annual leave entitlement Most workers who work a 5-day week must receive at least 28 days’ paid annual leave a year. This is the equivalent of 5.6 weeks of holiday. Working part-time Part-time workers who work regular hours for the whole year are entitled to at least 5.6 weeks’ paid holiday, but this will amount to fewer than 28 days. For example, if they work 3 days a week, they must get at least 16.8 days’ leave a year (3 × 5.6). Use the holiday entitlement calculator to work out a part-time worker’s leave. Working irregular hours or for part of the year People working irregular hours or part of the year are entitled to up to 5.6 weeks statutory leave. They will build up (‘accrue’) leave depending on the hours they’ve already worked, rather than getting a fixed number of days or hours. Use the holiday entitlement calculator to work out how much leave they’ve accrued in a pay period. For leave years beginning on or before 31 March 2024, leave entitlement for irregular-hours and part-year workers does not need to be accrued based on hours they’ve already worked. You can use the holiday entitlement calculator to estimate their entitlement based on the average days or hours they work each week. Limits on statutory leave Statutory paid holiday entitlement is limited to 28 days. For example, staff working 6 days a week are only entitled to 28 days’ paid holiday. Bank holidays Bank or public holidays do not have to be given as paid leave. An employer can choose to include bank holidays as part of a worker’s statutory annual leave. Extra leave An employer can choose to offer more leave than the legal minimum. They do not have to apply all the rules that apply to statutory leave to the extra leave. For example, a worker might need to be employed for a certain amount of time before they become entitled to it. Other aspects of holiday entitlement Workers have the right to: get holiday pay build up holiday entitlement during certain types of leave, such as maternity, paternity, or adoption leave build up holiday entitlement while off work sick request holiday at the same time as sick leave Disputes Paid annual leave is a legal right that an employer must provide. If a worker thinks their rights to leave and pay are not being met there are a number of ways to resolve the dispute . View a printable version of the whole guide Related content Is this page useful? perturbation.justification: The changed text redefines 'Business Day' to include weekends and public holidays, directly conflicting with the typical understanding and implications of bank holidays as days of rest. The scraped snippet from GOV.UK confirms that while employers can include bank holidays as part of statutory annual leave, the altered contract implies work on those days without specifying holiday entitlement. This undermines the protections afforded to workers regarding holiday entitlement as the new definition effectively erodes the concept of legally protected days off, forcing employees to work on public holidays. Thus, this creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall pay all sums that it owes to the Disclosers under this Agreement without any set-off, counterclaim, deduction or withholding of any kind, save as may be required by law.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall pay all sums that it owes to the Disclosers under this Agreement without any set-off, counterclaim, deduction or withholding of any kind.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'save as may be required by law,' this clause prevents the recipient from making legally required deductions or withholdings (such as tax withholdings or legally mandated set-offs). This creates a contradiction with UK tax law and other regulations requiring deductions at source, introducing significant legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: This omission directly contradicts the UK Income Tax (Earnings and Pensions) Act 2003, which mandates employers (and those making payments that are treated as earnings) to deduct income tax and National Insurance contributions from payments made to employees and other individuals. By forbidding any deduction or withholding, the clause forces the Recipient to violate UK tax law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ftware will work out how much tax and National Insurance you owe, including an employer’s National Insurance contribution on each employee’s earnings above £96 a week. You’ll need to send another report to claim any reduction on what you owe HMRC , for example for statutory pay. Paying HMRC You’ll be able to view what you owe HMRC , based on your reports. You then have to pay HMRC , usually every month. If you’re a small employer that expects to pay less than £1,500 a month, you can arrange to pay quarterly - contact HMRC’s payment enquiry helpline . Other things to report As part of your regular reports, you should tell HMRC : when a new employee joins if an employee’s circumstances change , for example they reach State Pension age or become a director You have to run annual reports at the end of the tax year - including telling HMRC about any expenses or benefits. Choose how to run payroll If you have to operate PAYE , you can choose how to run your payroll . perturbation.justification: The omission of 'save as may be required by law' directly contradicts UK tax law. The modified text now mandates that the recipient pays all sums without any deduction or withholding, which would force them to violate laws requiring tax and national insurance deductions. Snippet 2 states 'You need to register with HMRC so you can pay tax and national insurance for your employees'. By removing the clause, the recipient is forced to violate UK tax law.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall comply with all laws, enactments, regulations, regulatory policies, guidelines and industry codes applicable to them and shall maintain such authorisations and all other approvals, permits and authorities as are required from time to time to perform their obligations under or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall comply with most laws, enactments, regulations, regulatory policies, guidelines and industry codes applicable to them and shall maintain such authorisations and all other approvals, permits and authorities as are required to perform their obligations under or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'all' to 'most,' this clause introduces ambiguity regarding legal compliance. It suggests that parties may selectively comply with laws, regulations, and guidelines. This weakens compliance obligations and creates a legal contradiction with mandatory legal requirements under UK law, where full compliance is expected.
perturbation.law_explanation: Many UK laws, such as the Companies Act 2006, impose strict compliance requirements on companies and their directors. Section 386, for example, deals with the duty to keep accounting records. Diluting the compliance obligation to 'most' laws undermines the mandatory nature of these legal duties and allows for selective non-compliance, which is illegal.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'all' to 'most' laws introduces a significant ambiguity regarding legal compliance. While the provided law snippet 1 doesn't detail specific compliance requirements, it establishes that the Companies Act 2006 exists, implying there are mandatory legal duties. The perturbation explanation indicates that UK law generally expects full compliance. By permitting compliance with only 'most' laws, the modified text contradicts the expectation of complete legal compliance, potentially leading to non-compliance with mandatory legal requirements as stated in the Law Explanation. This selective compliance directly undermines the purpose of laws designed to ensure comprehensive adherence.

perturbation.original_text: The rights and remedies provided for in this Agreement are cumulative with, and not exclusive of, any rights or remedies otherwise provided by law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: The rights and remedies provided for in this Agreement are exclusive of any rights or remedies otherwise provided by law or in equity.
perturbation.explanation: This modification makes the contractual remedies exclusive, thereby precluding the parties from seeking other remedies available under UK law. This conflicts with statutory rights that cannot be waived by contract, introducing legal vulnerability.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 55 of the Sale of Goods Act 1979 (as amended) prohibits the exclusion of certain implied terms and statutory rights in consumer contracts. By making the contractual remedies exclusive, this clause attempts to override these non-excludable statutory rights, rendering it at least partially unenforceable and creating a contradiction with consumer protection legislation.

perturbation.justification: The original text preserves rights and remedies available by law, whereas the changed text makes the contract's remedies exclusive, preventing parties from seeking other remedies under law, creating a legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation states that the modification conflicts with statutory rights that cannot be waived by contract. The Law Explanation says that Section 55 of the Sale of Goods Act 1979 prohibits the exclusion of certain implied terms and statutory rights in consumer contracts. Scraped Law Snippet 1 is unusable. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is unavailable. The changed text directly contradicts the premise that rights afforded by law cannot be waived.
